title: Integrating statistical physics and machine learning for combinatorial optimization

data_scope.graph_sizes: not explicitly stated in Research Briefing; see companion article doi:10.1038/s43588-025-00782-0
data_scope.data_availability: no data availability statement in this briefing; experimental details in companion article
data_scope.code_availability: not stated in this briefing; uses standard ML autodiff + GPU libraries

core_conclusion: The free-energy machine (FEM) unifies statistical-physics free-energy minimization with ML gradient descent into a GPU-parallelizable solver that handles multi-valued variables and higher-order interactions natively, without requiring QUBO reformulation, outperforming specialized state-of-the-art algorithms on Max-Cut, balanced minimum cut, and MAX-k-SAT benchmarks.
why_it_matters: FEM breaks the QUBO bottleneck of Ising machines; inherits GPU acceleration from ML training stacks; achieves general-purpose performance without sacrificing competitiveness against specialized solvers; reveals deep structural correspondences between physics dynamics and ML optimizers; has direct reach to chip verification and logistics applications.
why_nature: Interdisciplinary synthesis of statistical mechanics and ML optimization matches Nature Computational Science's cross-domain priorities; a single method beating specialized solvers across multiple COP classes has broad audience appeal; using off-the-shelf ML infrastructure lowers barriers to high-performance computing.
added_value_to_my_research: FEM's facility-location reformulation -> probabilistic, uncertainty-quantified EV/healthcare site assignments replacing heuristic solvers
FEM marginal distributions -> spatial uncertainty maps for explainable spatial planning and policy communication
GNN-variational FEM ansatz -> physics-informed graph learning for transferable accessibility network solvers
FEM hybrid with photonic Ising -> GPU+hardware optimization for large mobility community detection and spatial clustering
Learned adaptive annealing -> meta-learned temperature scheduling for Monte Carlo/SIR agent-based spatial epidemic models

related.title: Free-energy machine for combinatorial optimization
related.relation: full companion research article providing technical depth and benchmark tables for this briefing

related.title: Programmable 200 GOPS Hopfield-inspired photonic Ising machine
related.relation: hardware Ising machine representing the class of solvers FEM's software approach generalizes beyond

related.title: Spatial planning of urban communities via deep reinforcement learning
related.relation: applies discrete combinatorial spatial assignment to urban planning — direct application domain for FEM

related.title: Many-body control with reinforcement learning and tensor networks
related.relation: RL + tensor-network optimization of quantum many-body systems — parallel physics-ML convergence to FEM's approach

related.title: Accelerating network layouts using graph neural networks
related.relation: GNN-based acceleration of graph optimization — illustrates the architecture needed for a learned variational ansatz extension of FEM

figures.caption: The principles and methodology behind how FEM solves COPs: (a) each variable adopts one of q distinct states with associated marginal probabilities; (b) FEM approximates the zero-temperature Boltzmann distribution via variational mean-field free-energy minimization with annealing schedule, decoding the ground state via argmax over marginal probabilities.
